focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The apartment smelled of sesame oil and sugar catching at the bottom of a pan. Anya stood outside with the key in her hand and thought, not for the first time, that David Crane had no business being in this hallway. He was there anyway. Sylvia's voice saying *Agent Crane is dead.* The border checkpoint. The gap in the coverage pattern that Anya had helped cut open with a lie.

Through the door she heard Bond's nails on hardwood and the low murmur of the radio Loid always kept on in the evenings. Yor moved in the kitchen. Water ran at the sink. Home sounded normal enough to make her throat close.

She should have called. There was a protocol for this. When an operation went bad and you needed a handler outside the chain, you filed a contact request and waited for a secure window. You did not show up at your parents' apartment at nine on a Tuesday with your hands shaking and a dead man's name lodged behind your teeth.

She let herself in.

Loid was at the sink in an apron, sleeves pushed to the elbows. Yor had the refrigerator door open and a bottle of sauce in one hand. Bond thumped once from the living room. For a second nobody had turned yet, and Anya saw the evening as it had been before she came in: dinner half finished, radio low, the kind of ordinary hour Crane would never get back.

"Anya!" Yor shut the refrigerator too fast. "You should've called. I would've made more rice. Have you eaten? Sit down. Wait, no, you look cold. Sit down first and then tell me if you've eaten."

Bond lumbered in from the living room and shoved his head against Anya's hip hard enough to make her stumble. The thought from him knocked into her in three blunt beats. *Home. Her. Good.* She bent to scratch behind his ears because it gave her one extra second before she had to...
open_hooks_to_carry: The SSS raid on Damian's intelligence cache — tipped by someone who knew where to look
Agent Crane killed in an ambush at a border checkpoint — death delivered by Sylvia's flat voice on a secure line
The causal chain: Anya's false report created gaps that Falk exploited — her lie contributed to Crane's death
Anya's compounding guilt: telepathy didn't save Crane; her lie may have killed him; her range and attention have limits
Anya and Damian in a cold basement — no comedy, the weight of failure
DAMIAN POV SECTION: he returns to his apartment, takes out the class photo, confirms Anya Forger is his partner
Damian's interior below surface thoughts — the things Anya's ability cannot reach — his reckoning with what this means about Eden Academy, about the friendship, about the kiss
He decides to continue the alliance — the stakes are too high to burn over identity — but he is no longer operating blind
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ce, and the social armor Becky wore so well most people forgot it was armor at all.

"You're my best friend," Becky said. "You know that, right? You're the only person who actually listens. Not the networking version. Not the I'll-pretend-so-you-invite-me-next-time version. You hear me. The real way."

*She's the only one who sees me. The Tuesday-night-cereal me. I don't know what I'd do without her.*

That landed harder than Damian's recognition.

This one was just Becky.

Since they were six, Becky had been talking to her twice: out loud and in the thoughts under the words. *I like her hair. I want to sit with her every day.* Since then Anya had taken the swallowed panic, the ugly little midnight inventories, the private parts Becky never chose to hand anyone.

Becky thought that was listening.

The truth pushed at Anya's teeth. *I've been hearing the rest since kindergarten.*

She swallowed it. Tell Becky now and every year of the friendship would go bad at once.

"You're mine too," Anya said. "My best friend. You know that."

It was true. It was also the most dishonest sentence she'd said all night.

Relief moved through Becky so plainly Anya could have touched it. Becky leaned her head against Anya's shoulder. Her thoughts quieted, not empty, just settled. A person who had finally said the hard part and been met where she needed to be met.

*Thank God for her.*

Anya stared out at the dark garden. The October air stung her eyes. Inside, the quartet had finally found an upbeat piece. Damian still hovered at the far edge of her range, careful and distant and impossible to unread.

Her pager vibrated again inside the bag at her wrist.

Border channel.

Becky didn't move.

Anya kept her shoulder where it was and looked out into the dark until the garden lights blurred.
secondary_character_beats: Sylvia delivering Crane's death should carry the institutional weight of someone who has done this before and will do it again
Damian's burned ally should feel like a real loss — a person who trusted him and is now endangered
state_deltas_required: the Renewal Front retaliates — a WISE agent is killed (partly due to Anya's false report), Damian's cache is seized, operational position collapses; Damian privately confirms Anya's identity

Major setback. Crane is dead, and Anya's false report is part of the causal chain. Damian has confirmed Anya's identity — the reader knows through his POV while Anya does not. The power asymmetry is no longer one-directional.
